skill.name: arch-view
skill.description: Generates architecture views from Backstage catalog metadata across repositories, including dependency graphs, request flows, event topology, and service groupings.
skill.summary: Create architecture diagrams and service maps from repository catalog metadata.
skill.author: AstraBit-CPT

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found most are false positives from Markdown Mermaid templates, Python f-strings, and documentation text. The skill is not malicious, but it includes local scripts that scan repository directories, write optional output files, and documentation that may invoke the GitHub CLI, so publication should include a filesystem and command-use warning.

medium_findings.title: Optional GitHub CLI Repository Discovery
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The skill documentation recommends optional use of gh repo list to discover repositories, which can contact GitHub and enumerate organization repositories. This is relevant to the architecture task, but users should approve the command and understand the scope.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command is explicitly documented and its purpose is repository discovery. It is legitimate for this skill, but it is still an external command with network-facing behavior.

medium_findings.title: Broad Local Filesystem Read and Optional Write Access
medium_findings.description: Verdict: TRUE_POSITIVE. The Python scripts accept user-supplied paths, scan repository directories for catalog-info.yaml files, parse local YAML, and can write generated output to a chosen path. This behavior is expected for the skill but requires user-controlled input paths.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly reads repository metadata and writes optional output files. I found no exfiltration or destructive behavior, so the concern is scope control rather than malicious intent.

low_findings.title: External Command Static Hits Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. Many static hits point to Mermaid or Markdown fenced examples and Python list construction, not shell execution. The reviewed Python scripts do not import subprocess, os.system, eval, or execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples are diagram templates and string assembly. No evidence found of shell execution in the reviewed Python implementation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The weak-cryptography locations do not contain cryptographic operations. They refer to metadata fields, prose, or diagram generation logic rather than hashing, encryption, or password handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no cryptographic API use at the reported locations. The static pattern appears to have matched ordinary words and diagram-related text.

low_findings.title: Certificate Or Key Static Hit Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The reported location iterates over dictionary keys while assigning a parsed YAML list. It does not access certificate files, private keys, tokens, or secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code context is a simple fallback YAML parser. No evidence found of credential discovery, key material handling, or secret file access.

low_findings.title: Obfuscation Static Hit Is A False Positive
low_findings.description: Verdict: FALSE_POSITIVE. The obfuscation hit points to a Python shebang and module docstring area. I found no encoded payload, bracket-chain obfuscation, dynamic import, or hidden execution path.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reported location is plain script metadata and usage text. The implementation is readable Python without obfuscated control flow.

dangerous_patterns.title: Documented External Command Use
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill may lead an agent to run gh repo list for organization repository discovery. This should require normal command approval and should be limited to the intended organization or repository set.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The external command is present in the workflow documentation. It is not hidden or malicious, but it can enumerate remote repository metadata.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Filesystem Paths
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts accept input and output paths from command-line arguments. Users should run them only against intended repository directories and review output destinations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The argument parsing and file operations are direct and easy to verify. The behavior is appropriate for the skill but still expands filesystem access.

content.value_statement: Large codebases make service relationships hard to inspect. This skill aggregates catalog metadata and generates diagrams and tables.
actual_capabilities: Aggregates catalog-info.yaml metadata from multiple local repositories.
Generates Mermaid service dependency graphs from dependsOn relationships.
Maps gateway request routes to downstream services and databases.
Builds event topology views from producer, consumer, and topic metadata.
Groups services by domain, type, and owner in Markdown tables.
Reports repositories that lack catalog metadata.
limitations: Requires repositories to expose useful catalog-info.yaml metadata.
Diagram accuracy depends on complete dependsOn, routes, and event fields.
GitHub repository discovery requires a configured gh CLI when used.
The fallback YAML parser supports only simple catalog structures.

use_cases.title: Map Service Dependencies
use_cases.description: Create a dependency graph that shows gateways, services, workers, databases, and their relationships.

use_cases.title: Review Request Paths
use_cases.description: Trace how API routes move from gateways to downstream services and persistent stores.

use_cases.title: Document Domain Ownership
use_cases.description: Group services by domain and owner for architecture reviews and onboarding documents.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Architecture View
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a complete overview.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an architecture view for the repositories in repos/. Include a summary, dependency graph, request flow, event topology, and missing metadata.

prompt_templates.title: Show Service Dependencies
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the catalog metadata in repos/ and create a service dependency graph. Highlight gateways, services, workers, databases, and missing links.

prompt_templates.title: Map Request Flow
prompt_templates.scenario: Troubleshooting API routing and downstream dependencies.
prompt_templates.prompt: Build a request flow map from gateway routes in the catalog metadata. Show route paths, downstream services, and database dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Domains And Events
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture review across ownership and messaging boundaries.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate domain groupings and event topology from all catalog metadata. Identify cross-domain dependencies, shared topics, and repositories with missing metadata.

output_examples.input: Generate a full architecture view for repos/.
output_examples.output: A report with repository counts, service type totals, dependency and request-flow diagrams, event topology, service grouping tables, and missing metadata notes.

output_examples.input: Show only event topology for the trading services.
output_examples.output: A focused event map showing producers, topics, consumers, and the services connected to each topic.

output_examples.input: Group services by domain and owner.
output_examples.output: Markdown tables listing each domain, its services, service types, and the owner recorded in catalog metadata.

best_practices: Run the scripts against a specific repository directory, not a broad home or system path.
Review missing metadata before treating diagrams as complete architecture documentation.
Keep catalog-info.yaml ownership, routes, events, and dependencies current.
anti_patterns: Do not use generated diagrams as security boundaries without validating runtime configuration.
Do not run optional GitHub discovery without confirming the target organization and access scope.
Do not rely on the fallback YAML parser for complex catalog files.

faq.question: What metadata does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses catalog-info.yaml fields such as component type, domain, owner, dependencies, routes, event producers, and event consumers.

faq.question: Does it modify repositories?
faq.answer: The aggregation script reads catalog metadata. It only writes generated output when an output path is provided.

faq.question: Can it discover repositories from GitHub?
faq.answer: The workflow documents optional GitHub CLI discovery. Users should approve that command and limit it to the intended organization.

faq.question: What diagram format does it generate?
faq.answer: It generates Mermaid diagrams and Markdown tables that can be rendered by compatible documentation tools.

faq.question: Will it work without PyYAML?
faq.answer: Yes, it includes a simple fallback parser, but complex YAML files should use PyYAML for better accuracy.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with warnings. The skill has legitimate filesystem and command-use behavior, but no confirmed malicious code was found.
